text: I set out on the Camino de Santiago with a heavy backpack and a vague idea of what lay ahead.
By the third day, my limbs ached and my motivation had begun to shrink.
A fellow walker — a retired nurse from Denmark — noticed my struggle and offered simple guidance.
Walk slowly, she said, and do not anticipate what comes next.
Her advice was profound in its simplicity.
Over the following weeks, the landscape shifted from green hills to dry plains and then to steep mountain passes.
Each morning I would emerge from a small shelter, stiff and sore but increasingly alert.
The solitude stripped away the noise of everyday life and exposed something underneath: a keen desire to live with greater intention.
By the time I reached Santiago, I had undergone a quiet transformation.
The journey did not provide answers, but it taught me to tolerate uncertainty — and that, I believe, was the whole point.
